text: The surgeon identifies the left ureter and opens the lateral space adjacent to it, known as the pararectal fossa, to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the origin of the uterine artery and begins to skeletonize it, preparing the vessel for the safe placement of a surgical clip.
The surgeon removes tissue medial to the uterine artery, revealing the ureter, which appears enlarged due to compression from the myoma.
The uterine artery is clearly visible, and the surgeon safely places a clip on the artery to control blood flow during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a grasper to assist in visualizing the ureter, which is positioned just below the instrument.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum on the right side, just lateral to the infundibulopelvic ligament (IPV), allowing the gas to reveal the right dissecting plane. The avascular retroperitoneal space is opened medial to the external iliac artery and vein, using traction and counter-traction to easily locate the internal iliac artery.
The surgeon removes fatty tissue surrounding the internal iliac artery and begins dissecting the paravesical fossa using traction and counter traction techniques.

The surgeon identifies the first medial branch of the anterior iliac artery, which is the uterine artery, followed by the umbilical artery. A clip is placed safely at the origin of the uterine artery.
The surgeon uses a devascularization technique to isolate the uterus, preparing to remove a large ten-centimeter type 2-5 myoma.
The surgeon opens the serosa surrounding the myoma, followed by the progressive opening of the myometrial capsule, after clipping both uterine arteries.
The surgeon demonstrates a bloodless procedure by controlling both uterine arteries to decrease uterine flow, using a harmonic scalpel to cut tissue with minimal bleeding.

The surgeon uses ten millimeter forceps to grasp and apply traction on the myoma, while the myometrial capsule is visible. The procedure remains bloodless as the hystereotomy is enlarged to accommodate the ten centimeter myoma.

The surgeon performs a seven-centimeter hystereotomy to facilitate the extraction of the myoma. The traction applied to the myoma maintains a dry operative field, allowing for clear visibility and precise manipulation.

The surgeon uses ten millimeter forceps to laterally grasp the myoma while cutting the surrounding tissue. The assistant retracts the normal serosa, and hydro dissection is employed, with the assistant using an aspirator to aid in the procedure.
The surgeon injects water between the uterus and the serosa to enhance visibility for dissection, focusing on the critical step of treating the myoma.

The surgeon focuses on the intracavitary part of the myoma, carefully applying energy directly to the myoma while sparing the surrounding endometrium. Minimal energy is used to avoid damage to adjacent tissues.
The assistants apply water to the residual endometrium to cool the area and decrease the heat generated by the ultrasonic scalpel during the procedure.
The endometrium is clearly visible, with techniques being employed to spare it and reduce the risk of post-operative senescence.
The surgeon identifies the correct dissecting plane, allowing for the preservation of the entire endometrium and the myometrial capsule during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses traction to remove the intracavitary part of the myoma, ensuring minimal disturbance to the surrounding endometrium.
The surgeon carefully avoids contact with the endometrium and surrounding myometrial tissue during the procedure, ensuring these structures remain intact.
The myoma is visibly removed from its bed, with care taken to spare the uterine cavity and minimize the risk of damage, as the surgeon proceeds slowly.
The surgeon identifies normal uterine tissue and carefully respects these structures while minimizing the use of harmonic energy. The uterus is visibly removed from its bed.
The surgeon performs a bloodless procedure, avoiding electrical hemostasis while directly manipulating the tissue.
The surgeon closes the myoma bed using stitches with a large 14mm needle, placing sutures two centimeters outside the uterine scar and securing them with an intracorporeal knotting technique.
The surgeon indents the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, ensuring careful manipulation to preserve tissue integrity.
The surgeon locks the knot using intracorporeal knotting technique, visibly securing the suture far from the myoma edges, achieving significant hemostasis.
The surgeon performs a reverse knot, taking a large bite of tissue to invaginate healthy tissues into the myoma bed, ensuring secure closure.
The assistants invaginate healthy myometrial tissue into the myoma bed, demonstrating effective anatomical reconstruction of the uterine structure.
The surgeon uses a V Quill suture to tie knots tightly, placing stitches at one-centimeter intervals to close the surgical site.
The surgeon carefully places stitches to restore normal anatomy and close the myoma bed, ensuring stitches are not placed near the fallopian tube.
The surgeon performs a double flat knot technique during the fertility surgery, with assistants locking the knot to ensure secure invagination of healthy tissue within the myoma bed.
The surgeon ties the final knot, positioned more laterally than previous knots, to invaginate the residual healthy myometrium. The knot is placed far from the visible myometrial scar.
The assistants close the knot and invaginate the healthy tissue inside the myoma bed, demonstrating the progressive closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon performs morcellation using a 15mm electromechanical morcellator in the left iliac fossa, with the device set to 700 volts per minute.
The surgeon continues the morcellation of the myoma under direct and permanent vision, showcasing the final aspect of the surgery.
